cognitive_dissonance.notes: Festinger (1957); deny sigmund_freud attribution — philosophy-style source discipline

left_brain_right_brain_pop.canonicalTitleEn: left brain / right brain personality dichotomy
left_brain_right_brain_pop.notes: Pop myth; split-brain research does not support personality hemispheres teaching

chemical_imbalance_slogan.canonicalTitleEn: depression is just a chemical imbalance
chemical_imbalance_slogan.notes: Oversimplified pop slogan; biopsychosocial clinical models are more accurate

stockholm_syndrome.notes: Not universal in all kidnapping cases — tag pop_myth when overgeneralized

learning_styles_vak.notes: Preference is not evidence-based instruction matching

dunning_kruger_effect.notes: Kruger & Dunning (1999) metacognition finding: low performers overestimate their skill. The pop reframe ('stupid people think they are smart') oversimplifies it — keep the cognitive subfield, do not merge with pop_myth.

confirmation_bias.notes: Peter Wason coined and experimentally tested 'confirmation bias' (1960s). Francis Bacon described the underlying tendency in Novum Organum (1620) but did not coin the modern term — credit the earlier description without misattributing the term to Bacon.

ten_percent_brain_myth.notes: Neuro-myth: humans do not use only 10% of the brain. Often falsely credited to Einstein or to a misreading of William James — neither stated the 10% figure. Neuroimaging shows widespread activity across the brain.

mozart_effect_myth.canonicalTitleEn: the Mozart effect (listening to Mozart raises IQ)
mozart_effect_myth.canonicalTitleZh: 莫札特效應（聽莫札特提升智商）
mozart_effect_myth.notes: Pop-science myth. Rauscher et al. (1993) reported only a small, temporary spatial-reasoning effect in adults; media inflated it into 'Mozart makes babies smarter'. Not a durable IQ boost.

ptsd_clinical_vs_pop.canonicalTitleEn: PTSD as a clinical diagnosis vs pop 'trauma' talk
ptsd_clinical_vs_pop.canonicalTitleZh: 創傷後壓力症（臨床診斷）與通俗「創傷」用語之別
ptsd_clinical_vs_pop.notes: PTSD is a clinical diagnosis with DSM-5 / ICD-11 criteria (trauma exposure, intrusion, avoidance, hyperarousal). Everyday 'that was so traumatic' pop usage is not a diagnosis — keep the clinical boundary; do not merge pop trauma slang with the clinical construct.
